feature_name: C64x+ DSP core and execution pipeline
description: The device integrates a TMS320C64x+ fixed-point DSP core designed for high-throughput signal processing. It supports efficient instruction execution with deep pipelining and specialized functional units. The core is tightly coupled with L1 caches and a configurable L2 memory to reduce latency. System events are serviced through a well-defined interrupt model to maintain real-time behavior. This core forms the computational backbone for all peripheral and DMA activity.
evidence: Section: 1) High-level architecture; Reference: SPRU732 (CPU/ISA)
dependencies: PSC domain for CPU/fabric must be active; clock via PLL must be stable
Remarks: Partition L2 and configure caches early to match workload characteristics.

feature_name: L1P/L1D caches and L2 SRAM/cache partitioning
description: The core provides separate L1P (program) and L1D (data) caches to accelerate instruction fetch and data access. L2 can operate as unified SRAM, cache, or a mix to balance capacity and latency. Proper MAR and cache mode configuration enables caching of external DDR2 regions. Cache configuration should follow memory initialization to avoid stale or incoherent data. Optimal partitioning reduces EDMA contention and improves real-time determinism.
evidence: Section: 1) High-level architecture; Section: 9) System bring-up (step 7)
dependencies: DDR2 initialized; PLL stable; PSC for memory subsystem enabled
Remarks: Lock cache strategy and MARs after DDR bring-up to avoid transient stalls.

feature_name: Power and Sleep Controller (PSC) module gating
description: The PSC manages power and clock gating for each peripheral and power domain. Enabling a module requires programming MDCTL[NEXT], issuing PTCMD.GO, and polling PTSTAT/MDSTAT for completion. Correct domain sequencing ensures dependencies are respected during bring-up. Disabling unused modules reduces dynamic power without impacting emulation-critical paths. The PSC state machine must not be bypassed to prevent undefined behavior.
evidence: Section: 2) Clocks and power (PSC); Registers: MDCTL, PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDSTAT
dependencies: PSC clock source active; device in a safe state for transitions
Remarks: Batch LPSC transitions per power domain to minimize latency.

feature_name: PLL clock generation and lock sequence
description: The on-chip PLL synthesizes CPU and peripheral clocks from a reference input. Programming follows a strict sequence: enter bypass, set PLLM/PREDIV/POSTDIV, issue PLLCMD for phase alignment, poll PLLSTAT for lock, and exit bypass. Timing constraints from the user guide must be observed to ensure stable operation. All downstream modules rely on these clocks for correct timing. Improper sequencing may cause intermittent boot or peripheral instability.
evidence: Section: 2) Clocks and power (PLL); Registers: PLLM, PREDIV, POSTDIV, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT
dependencies: Reference clock present; power rails stable; PSC domains awaiting valid clocks
Remarks: Apply divider changes only in bypass to avoid glitch propagation.

feature_name: Device configuration and pin multiplexing (PINMUX/KICK/DEVSTAT)
description: Device pins are multiplexed among peripheral functions and GPIO, selected via PINMUX registers. Writes to critical configuration registers may require unlocking KICK registers. DEVSTAT provides boot mode and input clock selection status at reset. Correct pinmuxing is prerequisite for enabling peripheral IO paths. Misconfiguration can disconnect signals even when modules are enabled.
evidence: Section: 3) Pin multiplexing and device configuration; Registers: DEVSTAT, KICKx, PINMUXn
dependencies: PSC for targeted peripheral; correct PLL frequency; board-level routing consistent
Remarks: Centralize pinmux settings and document shared pins to prevent conflicts.

feature_name: Boot ROM boot modes and DEVSTAT decoding
description: The Boot ROM selects a boot path based on strap pins reflected in DEVSTAT. Supported modes include serial peripherals and host interfaces depending on device configuration. Software can read DEVSTAT to adapt initialization or diagnostics. When booting to RAM, firmware is responsible for complete device bring-up. Understanding boot mode interactions simplifies recovery and manufacturing flows.
evidence: Section: 9) System bring-up (Early boot); Registers: DEVSTAT; Doc: SPRS371 (boot tables)
dependencies: Stable clocks and power; chosen boot interface pins correctly muxed
Remarks: Log DEVSTAT early to aid field debug and board bring-up.

feature_name: Interrupt mapping with Event Combiner and INTMUX
description: Peripheral system events are routed through the Event Combiner and mapped to CPU INT4–INT15 using INTMUX. The Event Combiner enables grouping and masking of many sources onto fewer CPU lines. Proper initialization clears pending flags and unmasks only required events. Mapping strategy impacts interrupt latency and service jitter. Consistent handling across ECM and CPU layers ensures reliable real-time response.
evidence: Section: 4) Interrupt architecture; Registers: EVTMASK/EVTSET/EVTCLR/EVTFLAG, INTMUXn
dependencies: PSC enabled for source peripherals; CPU IER/GIE configured; vector table valid
Remarks: Reserve distinct INTx lines for high-priority, low-latency events.

feature_name: CPU interrupt control (IER/IFR/GIE) and vectoring
description: The CPU level interrupt controller uses IER to enable and IFR to latch flags. Global enable (GIE) gates overall interrupt acceptance. Vector table setup and clearing of pending flags are required before enabling sources. Coordination with the Event Combiner prevents spurious entries. Proper sequencing ensures deterministic entry to ISRs with minimal overhead.
evidence: Section: 4) Interrupt architecture; CPU: IER, IFR, ISTP, GIE (SPRU732)
dependencies: INTMUX and ECM configured; memory for vectors accessible; caches coherent
Remarks: Place critical ISRs in L2/L1P to minimize latency.

feature_name: EDMA3 controller: PaRAM programming and transfer types
description: EDMA3 provides event-triggered, manual, linked, and chained transfers via PaRAM sets. Each PaRAM controls source/destination, indexing, and counts with options for completion signaling. Initialization involves enabling PSC, clearing events, assigning resources, and programming Q/Ch maps. Completion and error handling rely on IPR/ICR and related registers. Proper channel configuration offloads the CPU and sustains high bandwidth to DDR2 and peripherals.
evidence: Section: 5) EDMA3 overview; Registers: PaRAM (OPT,SRC,DST,A/B/C CNT,BIDX,LINK,CIDX,CCNT), IPR/ICR, ESR/EESR
dependencies: PSC enabled for EDMA3 CC/TC; clocks stable; memory targets ready
Remarks: Use chaining for pipelined peripherals and linking for descriptor reuse.

feature_name: EDMA3 event mapping, chaining/linking, and completion interrupts
description: Peripheral events can be mapped to EDMA channels to trigger autonomous transfers. Chaining enables one transfer’s completion to start another, while linking updates PaRAM sets without CPU intervention. Interrupts signal completion through IPR/IPRH with corresponding clears in ICR/ICRH. Correct event-to-channel mapping avoids contention or starvation. Robust servicing prevents queue buildup and latency spikes.
evidence: Section: 5) EDMA3 overview; Registers: DCHMAP, DMAQNUM, QCHMAP, IPR/IPRH, ICR/ICRH
dependencies: Interrupts configured; source peripheral enabled and pinned out; DDR2 ready
Remarks: Separate high-rate streams across TCs/queues to balance bandwidth.

feature_name: DDR2/mDDR controller initialization and timing
description: The DDR2 controller requires programming geometry and timing via SDCFG and SDTIM registers. Initialization includes refresh configuration and JEDEC-compliant sequence before normal operation. Proper PSC enable and PLL-derived clocking are prerequisites. Verification with memory tests confirms stability and integrity. Mis-tuned timing can cause intermittent faults under DMA/CPU load.
evidence: Section: 7) DDR2/mDDR Controller; Registers: SDCFG, SDTIM1, SDTIM2, SDREF, SDRSTAT
dependencies: PSC for DDR2 enabled; PLL stable; board DDR topology known
Remarks: Revisit timings across PVT corners and with cache/EDMA stress tests.

feature_name: GPIO controller: direction and data access
description: GPIO pins support input/output modes with DIR controlling direction. Output values are driven via OUT_DATA/SET_DATA/CLR_DATA, and inputs are read through synchronized IN_DATA. Availability depends on pin multiplexing choices. Correct PSC and pinmux setup must precede GPIO access. Banked organization affects how interrupts and status are aggregated.
evidence: Section: 6) GPIO functional summary; Registers: DIR, OUT_DATA, SET_DATA, CLR_DATA, IN_DATA
dependencies: PSC for GPIO enabled; PINMUX selects GPIO function
Remarks: Abstract GPIO access behind a thin HAL to isolate pinmux dependencies.

feature_name: GPIO edge-triggered interrupts and bank handling
description: Per-pin rising and falling edge detection is configured via SET_RIS_TRIG/CLR_RIS_TRIG and SET_FAL_TRIG/CLR_FAL_TRIG. Bank interrupts are enabled with BINTEN and sources identified in INTSTAT01/INSTAT. Proper ISR clears flags to prevent retriggering. Events can optionally synchronize EDMA transfers per device mapping. Correct mapping into INTMUX and ECM preserves low-latency service.
evidence: Section: 6) GPIO functional summary; Registers: SET_RIS_TRIG, CLR_RIS_TRIG, SET_FAL_TRIG, CLR_FAL_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01
dependencies: PSC for GPIO; INTMUX/ECM configured; optional EDMA channel mapped
Remarks: Debounce in hardware or software for noisy inputs to reduce ISR rate.

feature_name: UART (16C550-compatible with FIFOs)
description: The UART provides 16C550-like programming with transmit/receive FIFOs. Baud rate is derived by programming DLL/DLH, with line format in LCR. Interrupts indicate TX/RX thresholds and error conditions via IER/IIR. EDMA can service data movement depending on integration. Proper pinmux and PSC enable are required prior to use.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (UART); Doc: SPRUF93; Registers: DLL, DLH, LCR, IER, IIR
dependencies: PSC for UART; PINMUX to UART pins; clock stable
Remarks: Fix the baud clock early and validate across board-level tolerances.

feature_name: I2C controller with arbitration/NACK handling
description: The I2C module supports multi-master and slave modes in standard and fast speeds. It reports arbitration loss, NACK, and stop conditions as events or interrupts. Proper sequencing manages start, address, data, and stop phases. Error handling routines must clear status and recover the bus gracefully. EDMA support may be used for larger transfers where available.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (I2C); Doc: SPRUF94
dependencies: PSC for I2C; pull-ups present; PINMUX to I2C pins
Remarks: Implement bus recovery on stuck lines and monitor NACKs for retries.

feature_name: SPI master/slave with EDMA support
description: The SPI peripheral operates in master or slave modes with programmable word length and polarity. Data movement can be interrupt-driven or offloaded to EDMA for throughput. Configuration includes clock rate, phase, and chip select behavior. Status and errors must be polled or serviced in ISRs to maintain flow. Proper pinmux ensures signals reach external devices.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (SPI); Doc: SPRUF92
dependencies: PSC for SPI; PINMUX to SPI pins; EDMA channel allocated (if used)
Remarks: Use EDMA for sustained transfers and tune chip select timing.

feature_name: McASP multichannel audio serial port
description: McASP supports TDM/I2S/DIT modes with flexible serializers and clocking. Configuration spans pinmux, AHCLK/ACLK routing, and timeslot mapping. EDMA typically services high-rate audio streams to minimize CPU load. Initialization must adhere to reset and enable sequencing defined in the module guide. Incorrect clocking results in framing errors and audio artifacts.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (McASP); Doc: SPRUF91
dependencies: PSC for McASP; audio clocks present; EDMA configured; PINMUX set
Remarks: Validate bit clocks and frame syncs with a scope during bring-up.

feature_name: PCI interface with EDMA integration
description: The PCI module supports master/slave transactions and can work with EDMA for data movement. Proper configuration includes BARs, interrupts, and timing per the PCI guide. EDMA enables high-throughput transfers without CPU polling. System event mapping connects PCI interrupts into the CPU framework. Careful setup is needed to avoid bus contention and ensure cache coherency.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (PCI); Doc: SPRUF86
dependencies: PSC for PCI; external PCI clocking; INTMUX/ECM mapped; EDMA channels available
Remarks: Coordinate cache maintenance around PCI DMA windows.

feature_name: UHPI host-port interface
description: UHPI exposes internal memory-mapped access to an external host. It facilitates rapid data exchange and control for co-processor use cases. Configuration aligns address windows and handshakes as per the module guide. Interrupts coordinate buffer ownership between host and DSP. Proper isolation from time-critical real-time paths is recommended.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (UHPI); Doc: SPRUF87
dependencies: PSC for UHPI; host-side timing respected; interrupts mapped
Remarks: Define clear ownership protocols to avoid race conditions with the host.

feature_name: 3-Port Gigabit Ethernet switch (3PSW) with SGMII and MDIO
description: The 3PSW subsystem integrates a managed switch fabric with SGMII ports and MDIO for PHY management. Packet movement typically uses DMA queues with dedicated descriptors. Proper initialization sets port states, VLANs, and flow control per system design. MDIO accesses configure PHY link, speed, and negotiation. Integration requires careful pinmux and clock planning.
evidence: Section: 7) Other modules (3PSW Ethernet); Doc: SPRUF97; MDIO reference
dependencies: PSC for 3PSW; SGMII/REFCLK as required; MDIO pins muxed; EDMA/DMA queues ready
Remarks: Stage link bring-up: MDIO first, then MAC/switch state, then traffic.

feature_name: System bring-up sequence (clocks, PSC, pinmux, DDR2, EDMA, interrupts)
description: A recommended sequence ensures stable initialization from reset. Configure PLL first, then enable essential modules via PSC, and apply pinmux settings. Initialize DDR2 timing and verify memory integrity. Set up EDMA resources and map interrupts through ECM/INTMUX, enabling CPU IER and GIE last. This ordering minimizes side effects and eases debug.
evidence: Section: 9) System bring-up and initialization sequence (steps 3–11)
dependencies: Boot mode known; power stable; access to device config registers unlocked
Remarks: Automate bring-up with clear checkpoints and assertions.

feature_name: Power management and emulation-safe gating
description: The device supports clock gating of unused modules to reduce power, but clocks needed for emulation must remain enabled. The IDLE instruction can be used between events to save power without losing context. Wake sources must stay ungated in PSC to function. Careful planning avoids gating segments required for memory access during halt. Emulation considerations take precedence during active debug sessions.
evidence: Section: 12) Power management tips and emulation considerations
dependencies: PSC policy defined; interrupt wake sources mapped; debugger in use
Remarks: Maintain a power map distinguishing production from debug settings.
